title: Exceptional jumps of Picard rank of K3 surfaces over number fields
speaker: Salim Tayou
abstract: Given a K3 surface XX over a number field KK, we prove that the set of primes of KK where the geometric Picard rank jumps is infinite, assuming that XX has everywhere potentially good reduction. This result is formulated in the general framework of GSpin Shimura varieties and I will explain other applications to abelian surfaces. I will also discuss applications to the existence of rational curves on K3 surfaces. The results in this talk are joint work with Ananth Shankar, Arul Shankar and Yunqing Tang.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic Noether–Lefschetz locus and main theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines K3 surfaces, Picard ranks and specialization maps at good reductions, formulates the Noether–Lefschetz locus of primes where the geometric Picard rank jumps, and states the main theorem that this locus is infinite under an everywhere-good-reduction hypothesis, with parity and density remarks explaining when the theorem is nontrivial.

proof_stages.label: Motivating consequences and parallel infinitude phenomena
proof_stages.summary: The theorem is placed in context through an application to rational curves versus supersingular reductions on K3 surfaces and through earlier analogous results for elliptic curves and abelian surfaces that exhibit infinite but often density-zero special reduction loci.

proof_stages.label: Complex Noether–Lefschetz blueprint
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews the complex-family version of the Noether–Lefschetz problem for K3 surfaces, including nonemptiness, analytic density, and equidistribution results, and frames the arithmetic theorem as a mixed-characteristic analogue.

proof_stages.label: Period map and special divisors for polarized K3 families
proof_stages.summary: For a polarized complex K3 family of generic Picard rank one, the weight-two variation of Hodge structure on the primitive cohomology yields a period map to an orthogonal Shimura variety, where special divisors exactly encode the appearance of additional Hodge classes.

proof_stages.label: Borcherds modularity and complex intersection estimates
proof_stages.summary: Borcherds’ modularity of the generating series of special divisors gives growth estimates for their intersections with the curve, while local equidistribution of normalized lattice points supplies the finer complex equidistribution picture.

proof_stages.label: Integral Shimura model and Arakelov height formula
proof_stages.summary: In mixed characteristic, the orthogonal Shimura variety and special divisors are extended to integral models, arithmetic Chow classes with Green functions are formed, and pulling them back along the K3 period map gives a height decomposed into finite local intersection terms and Archimedean Green-function terms, with modularity bounding the total height by O(m^{b/2}).

proof_stages.label: Asymptotic estimates and final contradiction
proof_stages.summary: Assuming only finitely many primes contribute, the speaker estimates the Archimedean Green functions as having order m^{b/2} log m for a density-one set of m and shows each fixed finite-prime contribution is o(m^{b/2} log m), contradicting the modular height bound and forcing infinitely many Picard-jump primes.

examples.name: Smooth quartic surface in projective 3-space
examples.note: A smooth quartic in P^3 is given as a concrete K3 surface, and later reappears in an audience question about whether such quartics can satisfy the everywhere-good-reduction hypothesis.
examples.engagement_reason: The smooth quartic in projective 3-space is cited as a standard example of a K3 surface, with only a brief aside about good reduction conditions and no concrete calculation or verification.

examples.name: Picard-rank-20 K3/Kummer surface from a CM abelian surface
examples.note: In response to questions about supersingular reductions, the speaker and audience mention that for Picard rank 20 a jump must be to rank 22, with Kummer surfaces from CM abelian surfaces as a familiar source of such behavior.
examples.engagement_reason: The Picard-rank-20/CM Kummer situation is invoked as an example where jumps would be supersingular, but the speaker does not compute or analyze that surface.

examples.name: Charles’s theorem on reductions of two elliptic curves becoming isogenous

examples.engagement_reason: Charles’s result on two elliptic curves becoming isogenous after reduction is stated and compared to the K3 theorem via products/Kummer surfaces, without working through a particular case.

examples.name: Everywhere-good-reduction abelian surface splitting after reduction
examples.note: The speaker states that the same method also gives infinitely many primes where an abelian surface with everywhere good reduction becomes geometrically isogenous to a product of two elliptic curves, illustrating the reach of the argument beyond K3 surfaces.
examples.engagement_reason: The everywhere-good-reduction abelian surface splitting after reduction is presented as another theorem yielded by the method, but the talk does not work out the construction for that example.

passes.reason: Everywhere good reduction is the minimal-bad-reduction extreme for an abelian surface, and the splitting result is presented as a positive consequence of the method.
An everywhere-good-reduction abelian surface whose reductions split is part of an infinite systematic setting and is presented as another analogous infinitude theorem.
